reasoning: The SOFR Index measures the cumulative impact of compounding the SOFR (Secured Overnight Financing Rate) over time. Because SOFR is an interest rate that is currently well above zero (around 4-5%), the compounded index increases monotonically every single day. The only way it could decrease is if the Federal Reserve adopted negative interest rates, which is extremely unlikely to happen in the US in the next month, or if there were an unprecedented data error/revision. Therefore, the index is practically guaranteed to be higher than its February 15 value on both February 22 and March 17. Following the strict instructions to increase uncertainty for distant horizons, I assign 98% for the near-term date and 97% for the further date, representing near-certainty in a monotonically increasing index.
